data.title: Unpacking Global Fiscal And Trade Imbalances
data.description:
Transcript

Ken Chambers: Global uncertainty, U.S. exceptionalism coming under question. And really this shift in priorities towards national interests. What were some of the discussion points?

Tiffany Wilding: Yeah. So clearly, we have a new potential disruptor.

And we would argue these various policy pivots, all kind of come back to the interconnected issue of global trade imbalances.

What the administration would argue is that efficient market mechanisms should effectively balance trade over time. So an example of that is if you have a country that has a competitive advantage in some product or service, they'll export, they have an initial trade surplus.

But as a result of that, they also are getting higher domestic incomes from all of those profits coming back into the country, and they consume more. And as they consume more, they import more things. You probably also have a currency adjustment and appreciation that's happening. And over time, that helps rebalance trade. So you effectively are exporting to import.

But if you look at the last 40 years of trade within the global economy, what you see is that mechanism clearly has not happened. So we have countries that have effectively run serial trade surpluses China, Japan, maybe before the pandemic, also places like Germany, Korea, Singapore against other countries in the global economy that run serial trade deficits, that's the United States. But also, you have countries like the UK and France that do as well.

So there's a key question around why is that happening, why are we not getting that rebalancing? And the administration would argue it's because of policies, regulatory, other sort of structural policies within these trade surplus countries that effectively depress consumption. They require their household sector to subsidize manufacturing. And so, the result of that has been places that don't do that, like the United States, they can't compete. And we've had a hollowing out of the middle class. We've had a reduction in manufacturing share in the United States because we can't compete with this. As a result of how the government's social safety net is set up, that's resulted in automatic stabilizer type of programs that have increased the US federal fiscal deficit. So things like Medicare, food assistance programs, US disability programs, all have had increased pickup as a result of what economists call this a China shock.

So, as you can see as I'm talking, these are structural issues that require structural adjustments that will be difficult. And I think that just puts in perspective the broader discussion that's happening in Washington around really cutting federal government spending and fiscal deficits.

So all of this is to say is that the fed has some tricky decisions that they're going to make. We think it really will boil down to our recession risks rising faster than inflation risks. And on this point, I would say that there has been recently a very uncomfortable increase in inflation expectations across the various consumer surveys and business surveys more recently, and this could eventually constrain the fed. It's obviously going in the direction they don't want it to go. We think what it means is the more preemptive cuts that the fed did in 2018, 2019, for example, against potential weakness, they could be more constrained in that. But nevertheless, if they're seeing the unemployment rate increase, we think they're cutting aggressively as those recession risks are increasing.

But the US is not the only place that needs to make these adjustments. Elsewhere, like China, other places also need to make those adjustments. The US is trying to force that through higher tariffs. That's creating some disruption. And it's really yet to be seen how much additional price level adjustment, how much additional market volatility the US voter base is willing to tolerate to get through this period. So I think all of this is to say, is that these structural adjustments, they have important constraints. And that's just creating a lot of near-term uncertainty.

Disclosure

data.title: On The Right Road With Intel's Financial Future
data.description:
Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) forfeited it's pristine and coveted standing on the top of the semiconductor pile some years ago. Some claim arrogance drove the fall; others believe that management stopped seeing the future (AI chips); and even others attribute it to an overweight on maintaining margins. Perhaps, it's some combination. Nevertheless, a prior CEO hammered a stake in the ground for its path forward, developing both premium products in its own state-of-the-art fab. Doubts abound and, in our view, completely miss a reality, divorce the two and die. It is that simple. This represents our second article, the first, Where Intel Might Be Heading Financially, explored future profitability. Is the right path still in store?

Understanding Intel's Terminology

Before we head into the financial and vision meat, the numerous number of company products drives us to include some summarizes from which a vision and score can be kept. Management thinks of its product business in three parts, edge, traditional data centers and AI (Can be data center oriented or more).

A table, not necessarily all-inclusive, summarizing processes begins this disclosure.

Process Summary * Intel 3 Intel 4 Intel 20 (Canceled) Intel 18A Intel 14A
Granite Check
Meteor Lake Check Check
Lunar Lake TSMC 3NM
Arrow Lake TSMC 3NM
Panther Lake Check
NOVA Lake Check plus TSMC
Grand Rapids Check Check
Clearwater Forest Check
Gaudi ???
Falcon Shores (Canceled)
Jaguar Shores Check

* Fab shop is moving rapidly to EUV processes.

Next, our table inclusion contains products by the three major businesses, computing (EDGE), data center and AI. The table is ordered loosely by time.

Business Products Products In Time
Edge Meteor Lake Arrow Lake Lunar Lake Panther Lake NOVA Lake (2026)
Data Center/Servers Grand Rapids Clearwater Forest
AI Gaudi Falcon Shores * Jaguar Shores (Future)

* Axed and now a test chip used to develop the next generation.

Some term definitions follow:

  • NEX - Network and Edge Group.
  • CCG - Client Computing Group.
  • DCAI - Data Center and AI Group.
  • IMS - Nanofabrication business.
  • Mobileye -Driver assistance.
  • Altera - field-programmable gate array provider.
  • SCIP - Semiconductor Co-Investment Program (Smart capital approach.)
  • Intel Products - incudes CCG, DCAI, and NEX.
  • Core Ultra - Integrated NPU & CPU.
  • Xeon® 6 - Server processors family.

This ends our company summary, hopely adding help for readers in keeping score. We fully welcome comments fixing errors or additions, more than fully welcome.

A December Quarter/Year Summary

Now, for the quarter, management reported:

  • Receiving $1.1 billion from the Federal Government in the last quarter from the CHIP ACT.
    • $1.1 billion in January.
  • 4th Quarter revenue of $14.3 billion. (high end.)
  • Non-GAAP margin above guidance at 42%.
  • Earnings at $0.13.
  • Positive operating cash flow at $3.2 billion. (Included a -$900 million from restructuring charges.)
  • $6.3 billion CapEx.
  • Intel Products was $13 billion up nicely qoq.
    • CCG revenue higher by 9% qoq.

For the year 2024, management continued:

  • Revenue of $53 billion.
    • Mobileye and Altera lower revenue.
  • Gross margin of 36%.
  • Earnings of $0.13.
  • Capital of $13.4 billion from SCIP and the U.S. government
  • FCF of $2.2 billion and a year ending cash of $22 billion.

1st quarter guidance included:

  • Soft on revenue of $11.7 - $12.7 billion.
  • Margin of 36%.
    • Improves during the year going forward but less than the 51% average in 2024 for Intel Products.
  • Zero earnings.
  • "Intel Foundry revenue roughly flat to down modestly quarter-over-quarter, helped by continued mix shift to EUV wafers, Intel 18A samples and advanced packaging."
  • 2025 CapEx of $20 billion, slightly lower than previously guided.
    • "We expect 2025 net CapEx of $8 billion to $11 billion, with roughly half of the offsets expected to come from government incentives and tax credits and half from partner contributions."

A couple of comments seem important. Management has been out visiting with a lot of customers, in particular for future foundry prospects. David Zinsner, CFO, noted,

"I come away from those meetings encouraged by the opportunity we have in front of us, and I have received clear feedback on what our customers need from us to succeed. This starts with our execution on Intel 18A."

And a richer definition of marketing success relies on:

"However, foundry wins are about more than just technology. Trust is also a significant factor. Customers must believe you can execute consistently and be willing to invest in IP to port a design to a new foundry."

Management noted a very healthy list for the RFQ pipeline.

A Strategy & Vision

Next, we turn to the future to help answer which turn. Beginning, the CFO, product manager and new CEO all offer this idea, "We are also continuing to simplify our business and become a leaner, more efficient company." We expect more layoffs.

Michelle Johnston Holthaus, head of the product group, summarized each segment:

  • Client edge - Intel CPUs power 7 out of 10 PCs.
  • Slow-growing market 3% - 5% with expectations for a Windows upgrade later in the year to increase growth.
  • Panther Lake being the first 18A product second half of the year. (On schedule.)
    • State-of-the-art process and product.
  • Nova Lake begins in 2026.
  • Competitive.

AI PC CPUs remain on track to sell 100 million units in total during 2025.

On the AI market, Intel must develop a significant higher level of technology in order to catch-up. Intel noted that Falcon Shores development morphed into an experimental test product used to develop Jaguar Shores. Management offered a clear and open description of the real nature of AI.

"AI is not a market in the traditional sense. It's an enabling application that needs to span across the compute continuum from data center to the edge. As such, a one-size-fits-all approach will not work, and I can see clear opportunities to leverage our core assets in new ways to drive the most compelling total cost of ownership across the continuum."

In this statement, Holthaus articulated the complex nature of implementing AI. Apple (AAPL) understands this best, in our view. Unlike others, Intel maintains within its product offerings the ability to reach into markets in an optimal manner. For example, the Core Ultra Family of products combines both CPU/NPU functionality on the same chip.

Continuing, with regard to the foundry, $15 billion worth of business is needed for breakeven. The company expects to achieve this by 2027. $15 billion is less than 10% of the market. At the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference, John Pitzer, Corporate Vice President of Investor Relations, added,

"[This] target of giving Intel foundry to op profit breakeven by the end of 2027 that's mostly on the back of Intel products and actually mostly on the transition from moving our mix from Intel 7 wafers to Intel 18A."

Intel possesses capabilities to achieve its own objective going forward. It is about time and diligence.

In the past several days, the company announced the hiring of a CEO, Lip-Bu Tan, a former member of the board. His experience seems critical for future success. He plans to continue the strategic direction laid out by the former CEO with a greater resolve to put in order costs. In his opening company comments, he stressed, "up our game" and gain competitiveness. This is truly a turnaround story with time needed.

Continuing with our investment call, we noted in Where Intel Might Be Heading Financially,

"Properly managed fab shops can produce 30% or more gross margins, with product margins in the 60% + range. For Intel, an overlap in margins will become apparent. With respect to margins, management commented,

"As our mix of outsourced products and CCG grows in calendar year 2025 and we ramp Intel 18A to support Panther Lake, gross margin expansion could be muted, particularly in the second half.""

And this:

"The 2030 target included $15 billion of non-Intel business at 35% margin equal $2.5 in cash earnings. What is this worth in value? Is it 15 times or 20 times?"

For a $20 minus stock price, Intel stands poised to make a lot of money. When or if, 18A succeeds, the value of company is several times higher. But, in this market, we would wait.

For 2025

The company views 2025 with a microscope for a major transition. Management guided only for a small profit. Tan expects collaboration between products and foundry for identifying growth. 18A development continues on or better than schedule. The tape-out process, the final step before production, begins in the next few months. Semiconductor companies don't tape out unless the process has been proven.

In summary, management noted,

"Yes. So, both good questions. On the 18A front, I would tell you that the key milestones is us launching Panther Lake in the second half of the year, which I said we're absolutely on track to do. I've also talked about taping out an external design in the first half of this year, which I think is another sort of important milestone."

Risks

Before we start discussing unique risks, our view is that buying should be stopped until the market settles. At the earliest, the coming investor conference dealing with 18A progress might be an entry event. The market is forecasting a recession though one might come it is likely mild, in our view. Now, Intel faces many risks going forward. Among these are successfully enticing outside fab business. It needs $15 billion yearly for breakeven achievable from in-house sources by 2027. Yet, the fab profitability by "2030 target included $15 billions of non-Intel business at 35% margin equal $2.5 in cash earnings." Profits and improved margins depend heavily on the company's ability to continue manufacturing silicon in-house and in particular using higher margined EUV technology. This reality should help those who want the two pieces split and understand how bad an idea this is.

Another risk lies within the catch-up in technology now required. Tan made that clear. Could existing business disappear from obsolescence? Certainly! But within the keys business, much of the obsolescence is dissipating. Analysts point out competitive markets pressuring margins, an environment not so present in times past. But again, with the fab/product synergy lacking elsewhere, Intel carries a massive advantage, massive.

The world's state-of-the-art process, 18A strongly appears certain. Tape outs are just around the corner. This is a game changer. This process plus a migration of Intel Products toward this process bodes extremely positive for the future. Packaging issues with the advanced Clearwater product delayed the timing for release. The issue has been resolved, rescheduling its launch into early 2026.

The major risk continues with short-term cash flows. On this issue, the CFO noted,

"We expect 2025 net CapEx of $8 billion to $11 billion with roughly half of the offsets expected to come from government incentives and tax credits and half from partner contributions."

Intel believes that a cash burn of $3 - $5 billion might occur. At the end of 2024, the balance sheet showed $50 billion in debt and $22 billion in cash. With 2025 expected to be low point for cash generation and usage, cash burns may end sometime in early 2026. Capital expenditure in 2026 will be lower, perhaps significantly lower.

The risk in our view is the cash burn. 2025 will determine the health of the patient moving forward. It appears muted, thus far. The most important milestone continues with the successful launch of 18A and the beginning development of 14A. Thus far, the results are positive. The company stills has a several year period in which to catch up with AI, but it also has the scattered pieces for developing full and fully integrated solutions through the whole from center to edge. Will anyone else even attempt this? We rate the stock a buy long-term, but not now. The markets are too uncertain. That is the key. The path must include the fab plus process success. We believe that it will bloom. Intel chose the correct route.

data.title: Auto Tariffs Force A Gear Change Across Supply Chains
data.description:
The Trump administration has formally implemented tariffs of 25% on imports of automobiles from April 3 and certain components from May 5 following a Section 232 review of the sector in 2019 and an updated view of imports, which “continue to threaten to impair the national security of the United States.”

The new filing provides additional details beyond the administration’s initial announcement including precise product and country coverage as well as provisions for further expansion of duties.

US imports of the vehicles covered by the review reached 8.1 million cars, trucks and SUVs in 2024, of which 3.7 million were USMCA-eligible. This represents a 10.9% year-to-year increase in total vehicles in 2024 versus 2019, matched by a 10.6% year-to-year increase in USMCA eligible imports in the same period.

The presidential proclamation also calls for further monitoring to “identify and impose tariffs on additional automobile parts,” suggesting the duties will be expanded in the future as was the case with steel and aluminum duties.

The duties are “in addition to any other duties, fees, exactions and charges applicable.” Those will include additional duties applied under the Section 232 programs for steel and aluminum and those applied under IEEPA to non-USMCA compliant content from Mexico and Canada. They don’t, however, include the newly announced reciprocal tariffs, which specifically exclude autos.

Duties on USMCA products, about 46.0% of US imports by vehicle in 2024, will be suspended until rules are put in place to only apply duties on the value of the non-US content in the product, indicating that Mexican and Canadian origin products only get a temporary reprieve.

Vehicle imports have fallen sharply in 2025, with imports from Canada and Mexico by unit falling by 9.2% year-over-year (days adjusted) in January and February combined. Other large importers, like the EU, Japan and South Korea have seen imports fall by 12.2%, 8.9% and 7.7% year-over-year in the same period, signaling a sharp decline in the trade of completed automobiles.

What's covered under the new duties

While carried out under the auspices of the Section 232 national security program, the inclusion of components in the list of products subject to tariffs as well as vehicles repeats a pattern seen in Section 201 “safeguarding” tariff strategies, including those applied to solar panels and washing machines to avoid just having assembly reshored.

Of the US$328.0 billion of imports covered by the new tariffs — calculated as all imports less those eligible for USMCA-compliant status and excluding computers — based on 2024 data, 44.8% are accounted for by vehicles and 55.2% by components.

Within components, the largest imports are from the mechanical parts, batteries and electrical controls categories. The list of parts includes the HS codes for computers, 8471, covering almost all personal computing devices as well as computers and computer parts used in cars.

The order specifies that the parts receiving the additional duty are those are that are “parts of passenger vehicles,” and so only a portion of the US$140.2 billion in computer imports to the US in 2024 would be eligible for the 25% rate under the automotive 232 (with the remainder being possibly subject to the other orders). Note that no computers entered the US under USMCA privileges in 2024, but that does not preclude manufacturers from importing under that status in the future.

Accurately determining the use of computer parts may prove a challenge for importers and US Customs. This means that importers of computers may face additional compliance costs, especially as many computer components could be considered “dual-use goods” and be able to be used in a variety of applications including automotive.

Parts imports will see the impact of the duties at different times depending on the part’s USMCA eligibility and when the processes for USMCA-compliant goods, which will pay the 25% duty on non-US content, are announced.

For example, mechanical parts, the largest category of imports by value (excluding computers) at US$73.7 billion in 2024, saw only 19.8% of imports enter under USMCA, indicating a larger impact than a category like springs, which saw 81.5% of the US$848.8 million in imports enter under USMCA. The parts duties cover everything from bodies and suspensions to car radios and wiring.

The countries with the largest share of part imports include Mexico and mainland China, which saw US$124.3 billion and US$68.2 billion of parts imports respectively in 2024. Parts supply chains saw strong sourcing increases in Mexico, increasing by 23.6% year-over-year, while imports from mainland China fell in 2024 by 9.9% year-over-year. This likely reflects the preexisting tariff pressures on mainland China and highlights last year’s trend toward moving production into the USMCA bloc.

Mexico and mainland China accounted for 35.2% and 19.3% of total imports in 2024 respectively, followed by Taiwan and Vietnam at 8.5% and 5.6% respectively. Taiwan and Vietnam’s growth came from computer components, however, and without those, their share of US imports fall to 1.8% and 1.8% in 2024 respectively — further illustrating the increasing importance of computers in car manufacturing.

The impact of pending tariffs can be seen in imports in January and February, where on days-adjusted terms imports from mainland China increased 9.1% year-over-year excluding computers, while imports from Mexico fell by 1.5% in the same period.

Imports of parts from Germany and South Korea also fell year-over-year, with German imports falling faster when computers are excluded. January and February 2025 saw similar declines in days-adjusted terms, with Germany and South Korea falling by 21.2% and 6.3% year-over-year excluding computers.

Exposure to auto parts exports varies

Importing countries have different exposure to new US duties as well, with predictably the US’s USMCA partners sending a large proportion of their auto parts to the US, excluding computers. Canada’s auto parts exports to the US accounted for 61.5% of their total exports in 2024, while 51.7% of Mexico’s exports had the US as the destination. Both these countries will have some of the burden removed temporarily by the USMCA delay, but may see a fundamental reshaping of automotive supply chains in North America.

Canada has responded with its own tariffs, announcing a 25% duty on non-USMCA compliant (CUSMA in Canada) imports from the US and 25% on the US content of USMCA-compliant vehicle imports from the US.

The share of EU countries' exports of parts to the US is relatively low, with Ireland seeing the largest proportion at 7.3% of their total exports of US$2.2 billion in 2024 excluding computers. Germany’s 2024 total exports of auto parts of US$141.7 billion (excluding computers) saw only 2.4% go to the US but may have an outside impact on the cost to repair automobile brands affected.

Auto part exports from mainland China are also only modestly impacted, with 7.1% of their US$52.1 billion in parts exports excluding computers headed for the US in 2024, while countries like Australia and Brazil saw 22.4% and 7.6% of parts exports head for the US in 2024 using the same calculations.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: A 90-Day Tariff Reprieve
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

President Trump announced a 90-day reprieve on reciprocal tariffs, reducing them to 10% across all countries except for China, on which the U.S. raised reciprocal tariffs even further, from 104% to 125%. President Trump’s announcement emphasized that the decision to single out China was because they had retaliated against his reciprocal tariffs, whereas other countries had tried negotiating with the U.S.

Note that steel, aluminum, autos, and non-USMCA goods from Canada and Mexico are still subject to 25% tariffs. President Trump reiterated that he will still be announcing sectoral tariffs in due course.

What drove the President's decision?

The decision to grant a temporary reprieve came after the S&P 500 had dropped 18.9% from its late February peak (almost entering bear market territory) and after the bond market started to show concerning signs of a "buyer’s strike," driving bond yields higher across the curve.

This final point around the bond market likely struck a nerve with the Trump administration. They have repeatedly emphasized their focus on bond yields and even celebrated last week when Treasury bond yields dipped below 4%. Low financing costs appear to be a key pillar of the Trump administration’s overall agenda, so the reversal in market trends (surging Treasury yields) undoubtedly caused significant concern in the White House. Additionally, there were some unsubstantiated fears that stress was starting to build in the financial system, with a few comparisons even made to the Global Financial Crisis. These concerns prompted swift action to reduce the policy-driven risks.

Ultimately, Wednesday’s tariff pause highlights that President Trump cares deeply about the health of financial markets, and his pain threshold is most likely centered around the performance of the bond market. This knowledge should provide investors with some comfort as they try to map out a playbook for this crisis.

Market response shows the need to stay invested

Markets surged on the news, with the S&P 500 climbing almost 10% and the Nasdaq up 12% - their biggest single-day gains since 2008 and 2001, respectively. Credit spreads tightened, the VIX fell sharply from 58 to 34, and 30-year bond yields reversed their spike from earlier in the day. European and Asian markets are following the U.S. higher this morning. Still, despite the historic rally, the S&P 500 remains 4% below its level prior to the Liberation Day announcements.

For investors, the tariff pause announcement, and subsequent market reactions reiterates the importance of staying invested in these volatile times, particularly when politics lies at the heart of the market disruptions.

After the sharp market swings of the past few days and a rapid drop in valuations, risk assets were primed for a strong rebound. If investors had moved into cash to wait out the volatility, they would have missed out on the tremendous upward move. Staying invested and retaining disciplined investment behavior are essential during periods of stress.

Impact on growth outlook... seeds of doubt are germinating

Now for the bad news. There are already a few seeds of doubt creeping back into the market, and U.S. equity futures are trading lower today as investors start to digest the fact that President Trump’s decision to raise tariffs to 125% on Chinese goods broadly offsets the positive impact of lowering other countries’ reciprocal tariffs to 10%.

Recall that, ahead of Wednesday’s announcement of a 90-day reprieve and a further increase in China's tariff rate, the U.S.’s average effective tariff rate had increased from 2% at the end of 2024 to 28%. After the announcement, the effective average U.S. tariff rate only falls to around 23% — still the highest level in over a century. Indeed, the 125% tariff on China represents a 19% effective tariff increase by itself. The upshot of this is that the U.S. economy is still likely to be hit meaningfully by import tariffs, and tariff-driven inflationary surges are still on the cards.

It is possible that the negative impact may be dulled somewhat if China can re-route its exports via other Asian countries, which now face 10% tariffs. Certainly, the very acute asymmetry in tariffs likely does allow for workarounds and trade diversion.

In last week’s bulletin, we noted that without deregulation, tax cuts, or a walk-back from import tariffs, the U.S. economy appeared headed toward recession. While the latest announcement has somewhat reduced the odds of recession, the risk remains elevated. A more substantial decline in recession risk would likely require a meaningful walk-back of the extreme tariffs on China.

Outlook for investors… as of today

Peak tariffs and, therefore, peak pessimism, has likely been reached. Knowing President Trump is watching bond markets also implies that the tail risk of liquidity strains morphing into a financial crisis has been cut back significantly and provides a floor from which market sentiment can recover.

That said, caution remains warranted. The potential impact on the U.S. is still significant, and uncertainty is likely to remain elevated through the 90-day grace period and as the U.S./China back-and-forth persists. Some damage to confidence — especially international confidence in the U.S. — has been inflicted and may prove lasting.

In times like these, the adage of staying invested with a diversified portfolio is more important than ever. Global diversification remains essential, especially given the asymmetric implementation of tariffs across countries, while cross-asset class exposure provides valuable resilience in periods of heightened volatility. After a sharp decline, equities have the potential to recover in the months ahead, and fixed income can help cushion ongoing economic risks that are still confronting the U.S. economy.

Most importantly, investors should remember that market pullbacks are not unusual — on average, the U.S. stock market experiences an intra-year decline of 13.5%, yet most years still end with gains of around 9%. Volatility is a normal feature of investing, not a flaw — those who remain disciplined and invested through the noise are often rewarded over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Taylor Wimpey: A Dividend Giant That Can't Be Ignored
data.description:
After Liz Truss's mini budget spooked housebuilder stocks into oblivion in late 2022, Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODY) made a rampant recovery in 2023 through to 2024. That being said, on the back of another disappointing Budget in October, the shares have tumbled back down, and are now trading below book value. But boasting a 9.0% dividend yield, here's why this stock can't be ignored.

Completions are Hitting a Floor

Taylor Wimpey announced its FY24 results not long ago, and unsurprisingly, it didn't impress. Home completions were down 2.4% to 10,593, while the Average Selling Price ('ASP') decreased 1.5% to c.£319K. This was due to an unfavourable sales mix, where c.49.0% of homes sold were under £300K, as compared to c.50.0% in FY23. As a result, revenue decreased 3.2% to £3.40B.

This poorer mix saw gross profit fall 9.5% to £649M, along with gross margin dropping 132bps to c.19.1%. This was also due to the lagged effect from the prior year's Work In Progress (WIP) houses, when build cost inflation was significantly higher. This then flowed through to EBIT margins being 349bps lower at c.9.8%, while EBIT stooped 28.6% to £334MM.

And although net finance income was largely unchanged, income from JVs turned negative. This was due to the £16M worth of cladding fire safety provisions, which added further downward pressure to Taylor Wimpey's PAT, which fell 37.1% to £220M. Consequently, basic EPS suffered 37.4% to hit 6.2p, with DPS per share also taking a slight bump to 9.46p from 9.58p.

Nonetheless, CEO Jennie Daly did guide for a slightly more upbeat year ahead. On completions, she gave a range of 10,400-10,800 (ex. JVs) for the year, which, assuming the mid-range, would equate to a c.6.3% improvement. At the same time, she estimates EBIT to come in around the current market consensus of c.£444M, also marking a slight jump of c.6.7%.

These figures are in line with the underlying trends seen in the FY24 report, as the company has started the year brightly with a much larger order book ex. JVs (8,021 homes vs 7,402 homes), and a higher net private sales rate (0.75 per outlet/w vs 0.67 per outlet/w). Meanwhile, build cost inflation is expected to remain in the low single digits from last year's c.1.5%.

Attractive Yielding Prospects?

However, as optimistic as these projections may be, it's worth noting that there are a number of caveats attached to the guidance that might've not been explicitly mentioned on the earnings call. One of which is that the outlook is contingent on a recession not occurring, alongside the labour market continuing to remain robust with positive real wage growth.

The second was the stamp duty deadline in March, which could've given a false sense of security, as a slew of homebuyers rushed to finalise transactions, leading to the recent strength in mortgage approvals data as well as the tick-ups in customer interest cited by management. Therefore, it may be too soon to conclude definitively that this strength will last throughout the year.

After all, this strength has been put into question in recent weeks, as President Trump's tariffs could have a wide-scale impact on global economies, and trigger recessions into motion. In fact, UK bank stocks have seen sharp declines in recent days, as investors alike anticipate a sharp pullback in borrowing—often a leading indicator of housing demand.

On the flip side, there may be an element of good news amidst the trade war drama. Most prominently, gilt yields—a leading indicator for mortgage rates—have crashed substantially, as investors pile into government bonds to seek refuge amidst the market volatility. This could, therefore, spur lower borrowing costs, and push demand and house prices higher.

Having said that, we must note that such a scenario may only be possibly provided the fundamentals (lower inflation, robust unemployment, and positive real wage growth) within the UK economy remain intact. As such, it's only safe to assess the outlook more reliably when the home builder releases its interim results in late July, with all eyes on cancellation and sales rates.

A Dividend Policy That's as Tough as Bricks

Regardless of the near-term volatility, though, what we're most intrigued by is the value proposition at play. The stock is currently trading c.15.2% below its net asset value, with an extremely attractive dividend policy. For context, Taylor Wimpey has pledged to return c.7.5% of its net assets, or at least £250M annually, which equates to a minimum yield of 6.7%.

Nevertheless, we should still note that dividend policies can always be paused/retracted if the situation calls for it. In fact, the board did so briefly, during the pandemic, and could do so again if an outright global economic meltdown ensues, as it may need to shield its balance sheet.

But in an upside scenario where we assume minimal impact on earnings from tariffs in FY25, Taylor Wimpey stock could have a forward yield as high as c.9.1%. And with the shares already trading quite a bit below their book value, we envision some level of downside protection, with the high dividend yield serving as a healthy hedge, provided dividends aren't paused.

Even so, a pause in payouts isn't our base case. This is because unlike the 2008/09 financial crisis, the group's financials are in a completely different state, with net cash of £526MM with minimal debt. So, when the dust settles eventually, as we anticipate countries to come to the negotiating table to bring tariffs down, Taylor Wimpey could be an impeccable value and dividend play.

But as we don't cover the stock, we refrain from issuing an official rating. That said, our model suggests an upside of c.21.7%, with a forward dividend yield of c.9.1%. This is derived by comparing Taylor Wimpey's key ratios to their historical and sector(s) averages, before averaging their resulting fair values altogether. Although, we stress that this is contingent on consensus estimates.

Price Model Taylor Wimpey Sector AVG 5Y AVG Fair Value
EV/EBITDA (10% wt) 6.6 10.0 8.8 149p
P/E (10% wt) 16.9 15.3 15.9 97p
FP/E (25% wt) 12.2 16.7 11.8 123p
PEG (55% wt) 0.7 1.5 0.3 132p
Fair Value 128p

***As a reminder to our readers, Taylor Wimpey's primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange (LSE:TW). Trading this stock on OTCMKTS may present liquidity risks, resulting in higher bid-ask spreads. For optimal execution and more accurate price discovery, we recommend assessing the stock on the LSE instead, where possible.

Explaining Our Approach and Model

When it comes to our valuation model, we employ a combination of multiples approach, rather than the infamous Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model, which many investment banks utilise. To explain, our methodology centres on utilising multiple valuation ratios with an emphasis on forward-looking earnings multiples:

  • EV/EBITDA (10% wt);
  • P/E (10% wt);
  • Forward P/E (25% wt);
  • (Forward) PEG (55% wt);

We prioritise forward earnings metrics because stock prices predominantly reflect future earnings expectations rather than past performance. Although, we include trailing ratios with lower weightings in order to provide analytical balance. This is to mitigate the effects of volatility or potential M&A activity.

We believe this approach delivers greater accuracy than a DCF, for the reason that it can account for critical factors such as stock buybacks more accurately. Furthermore, DCFs typically produce significantly higher standard deviations, as minor adjustments to the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (OTC:WACC) and/or discount rates can dramatically alter price targets.

In addition to that, DCF models often contain “black box” elements with inadequate explanations regarding the discount rate, WACC determinations, and more crucially, terminal value calculations from perpetual growth rates. On the other hand, our combination of multiples model is more transparent, and more digestible for investors.

Multiples Appendix

  1. EV/EBITDA (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to FY24 results.
    • Market cap data for EV is calculated by multiplying shares outstanding as of the latest RNS with the latest share price.
    • EBITDA is calculated from: EBIT + Depreciation and Amortisation.
    • The sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes positive EBITDA firms.
  2. P/E (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to FY24 results.
    • EPS is calculated from basic EPS on profit after tax, divided by basic shares outstanding as of the latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes money-making firms.
  3. FP/E (25% wt)
    • Usually calculated by using weighted forward projections if the current period falls between fiscal years.
    • For example, if the current TTM is to H1'25, the calculation will be: (FY25 projected EPS—H1'25 EPS) + (FY26 projected EPS / 100 x historical weight of H1 EPS on FY EPS).
    • EPS is calculated from basic EPS on FTM profit after tax, divided by projected basic shares outstanding.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by S&P Global Market Intelligence.
  4. FPEG (55% wt)
    • First, CAGR is calculated as: (FY27 EPS / TTM EPS) ^ (1 / 3 yrs)—1.
    • Then, FPEG is calculated as: (Current share price / TTM EPS) / CAGR.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Restaurant/Dining sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Don't Mess With The Bond Market
data.description:
The thrills and spills of the new world order (such as it is or may be) continued this week, with some of the wildest swings in equity markets since 2008. As much as those gyrations in the Dow and the S&P 500 dominated the financial news shows, though, the real action was taking place in the bond market.

It was there, in what is often called the plumbing of the global financial system, that a sudden spike in Treasury yields midweek led to the temporary “pause” on the novel and ill-considered tariff program announced last week.

Vigilantes, Of a Sort

A few weeks ago, we made reference in this space to the “bond vigilantes” of a few decades ago – the likes of Salomon Brothers economist Henry Kaufman and his ilk, who bet against bonds (sending yields higher) in reaction to what they considered irresponsible fiscal chicanery coming out of Washington.

What we saw this week in the bond market was a bit of vigilante-ism, giving a thumbs-down to the budding trade war, but also something potentially even more concerning.

Because Treasury securities are highly liquid, they are relatively easy to dispose of in the market when you need to raise cash quickly. This week, a number of institutional investors needed to do just that. Plummeting stock prices hastened margin calls, leading financial players highly exposed to short-term market disruptions to unload Treasuries.

The spike in yields, shown in the chart above, caused a break in the normally stable fabric between spot yields and Treasury futures for commensurate maturities. Hedge funds are a major player in arcane strategies that aim to profit from the relative value movements between spot and futures prices.

When those movements go wonky, as they did earlier this week, bad things happen. The mechanics are complex, but the net outcome is that more Treasuries get sold, and then yields keep going up, and then more needs to get sold in what can turn into a very nasty doom loop.

Shades of 2020

The last time things got this bad in the Treasury market was in March 2020, at the height of the Covid panic. The S&P 500 had fallen by 34 percent from its recent high on March 23 of that year, when the Fed stepped in with a fire hose of liquidity to stabilize markets.

But the Fed wasn’t there because of the equity market plunge. It was there because the Treasury market was about to blow up, and the consequences of that would be far, far worse than any damage stock prices could cause. It worked – both stocks and bonds stabilized after the Fed’s intervention, and the “everything rally” in risk assets began.

This time around, though, the root cause of the market disruption was not an exogenous event like a health pandemic. It was a policy decision that had the potential to severely damage the global trading system – a decision of human construction that could be reversed as easily as it was put in place.

The “pause” announced Wednesday wasn’t great – it did not include China (and the problem with that is a story in and of itself), it is only for 90 days, and it still leaves us with a much higher average rate of tariffs than we had at the beginning of the year. But it was enough, for the moment at least, to reduce tension in the bond market to a manageable level.

A Lesson About Short-Term Market Moves

You have heard us say over and over again that short-term movements in the market are unknowable. In times of unusually high volatility, like now, the big intraday swings can be either up or down.

When the S&P 500 racked up more than twelve percent in losses in the four trading days after the April 2 tariff announcement, the limbic brain was urging sell, sell, sell! But then came the backtrack on April 9, and whoosh – up goes the index by 9.5 percent in less than four hours of trading.

The point is – things are going to happen, and we aren’t going to know what those things are until they happen, and they are going to move prices both up and down, often instantaneously. Trying to outguess any of this is a futile exercise. Stay disciplined. As for us, we will, to paraphrase Metallica, sleep with one eye open… on the bond market.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SCHY: A Flawed Fund That Should Continue To Underperform
data.description:
There are many different types of investing that can be successful. While certain financial approaches tend to be more successful, individuals have used many various philosophies to achieve their financial goals.

An increasingly common forms of investing over the last decade has been to focus on dividends. A growing number of exchange-traded funds and investments have come to market over the last decade designed to provide individuals with substantive and consistent income, with rates being at historic lows for much of the last 17 years since the financial collapse in 2008.

One exchange-traded fund focused on dividends that recently were listed in 2021 is the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHY).

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF has offered investors total returns of 6.50 percent since the fund's inception in April of 2021. The S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 25.15 percent during the same time period.

Today, I am initiating my coverage of the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF with a sell rating. SCHY's core positions have consistently and significantly underperformed industry averages and the broader indexes. The ETF also has significant exposure to the current trade conflict with China and should have forex headwinds as well.

SCHY has an expense ratio of .08 percent, a trailing yield of 4.55 percent, and $856 million in assets under management. The ETF's holdings 16.02 percent consumer defensives, 14.91 percent financials, 13.42 percent communication, 12.03 percent health care, 11.55 percent the industrials, 7.61 percent utilities, 7.17 percent basic materials, 709 percent energy, 7.03 percent consumer cyclical, 2.33 percent technology, and .85 percent in real estate.

Most of SCHY's positions are in 10 countries.

This Schwab fund uses four different criteria to find stocks. The managers of this fund look at cash flow to debt, return on equity, indicated dividend yield, and 5-year dividend growth. Companies must have at least 10 years of dividend growth. SCHY seeks to track the Dow Jones 100 International Dividend Index, which is comprised of companies outside of the US, and the ETF reconstitutes holdings in March, once a year. SCHY's 4 largest holdings are Enel SpA (OTCPK:ENLAY), Unilever PLC (UL) Ono Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:OPHLF), and British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI). These 4 positions make up nearly 15 percent of the ETF. SCHY's year-end yield over the last 3 years has been between 4 percent and 4.66 percent.

The main problem with SCHY is the fund has very narrow criteria which has forced managers to allocate capital to lower-quality positions and has led this investment to consistently underperform both the broader indexes and most peers as well. This Schwab fund has significantly underperformed the Vanguard International High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYMI), the Franklin International Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF (LVHI), and the WisdomTree International High Dividend Fund ETF (DTH), over the last 3 years.

The core problem with this Schwab ETF is the number of underperforming companies that the fund holds as core positions, such as Unilever, British American Tobacco, and Enel Spa.

SCHY also has significant exposure to forex moves, and despite the recent sell-off in the dollar against most major currencies, the dollar is likely to rise over the next year with the Fed remaining more cautious than European and Chinese Central Banks, the US economy remaining clearly the strong economy worldwide, and Trump determined to cut spending and use tariffs and other economic policies to bring manufacturing back to the United States. The dollar has been in a general uptrend over the last decade. SCHY also has significant exposure to the current trade conflict between the US and China.

All investment theses have risks, and SCHY would outperform if the dollar were to fall significantly against the Euro and other major currencies if the Fed became more dovish or Trump escalated the trade conflict between the US and other nations. SCHY has also consistently paid out over 4 percent a year, even though the fund's total returns have underperformed both the benchmarks and other peers.

There has been a significant increase in the number and types of funds that dividend investors can choose from today. While those seeking income were often forced to allocate capital to individual companies in the past. These investors have far more options today. Still, not all funds are created equally. While SCHY has consistently paid over 4 percent a year, investors should be able to find more appealing options in the current economic environment.

data.title: As The South Korean Market Crumbles, Coupang Is Worth Watching
data.description:
I wrote about KT earlier this week. That’s not a plug, but rather it got me thinking a lot about South Korea and the flight to safety toward telecoms on the major stock market drop we’ve seen there, and indeed we’ve seen most everywhere. Today I thought I’d stick with South Korea, but take a look at a once popular growth stock there, Coupang (NYSE:CPNG). They’ve had hard times lately, but that’s no surprise.

Coupang has been growing like gangbusters for years, and we’ll be looking at the growth under the lens of the current, relatively cheap entry point. We’ll be looking at their success this year (on a non-GAAP basis) and also reconciling the very confusing GAAP bottom line this year, which was related both to tax differences and how South Korea handles a fine levied that’s still under appeal.

We’ll be having a look at their avenues for growth, and also the potential risk that surrounds them based on the reality that they’re an American corporation operating in South Korea during the global tariff crisis.

Understanding Coupang

If we were in South Korea, you’d all immediately know who we’re talking about here. It might not be immediately clear for investors elsewhere, however, so as a brief primer on the matter, Coupang envisions itself as becoming the “Amazon of South Korea.”

Their business has many of the things you’d be familiar with from that goal, from a major shopping network, their own delivery system, even a premium service branded WOW which functions similarly to Amazon Prime, faster free delivery and video content and such. Beyond the obvious shopping service, they do Coupang Eats, which is a sort of DoorDash type service, a grocery delivery service like Instacart, a payment system, etc. Pretty much every e-commerce thing you can think of, Coupang is trying to do in South Korea in one form or another.

Beyond that, Coupang is looking to expand into other countries, with Coupang Taiwan on small but growing effort, which we will discuss later. In January 2024, they also acquired Farfetch Holdings, a marketplace service for the luxury fashion industry. That too we will consider later. First though let’s break down the finances.

2024 Broadly Successful, Despite Complexities of Numbers

2023

2024

Net Revenue

$24.4 billion

$30.3 billion

Operating Income

$473 million

$436 million

Income Tax Expense

($776 million)

$407 million

Non-GAAP EBITDA Margin

4.4%

4.5%

Net Income

$1.36 billion

$154 million

GAAP diluted EPS

75¢

8¢

Non-GAAP EPS

26¢

22¢

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

Here we can see some of the complexities of Coupang’s numbers in 2024. You’re not reading it wrong, they booked a $776 million gain on income taxes in 2023 and an expense of $407 million in 2024. That’s because of an unusual change of valuation allowances amounting to around $1 billion. In this case, we can see where the non-GAAP version, which doesn’t include such one-off issues, might be more indicative of where Coupang is really going.

The difference in GAAP and non-GAAP in 2024 is centered primarily around $127 million in acquisition-related costs for the Farfetch acquisition and a $121 million fine by the KFTC for alleged violations of the Monopoly Regulation and Fair Trade Act. That fine is still subject to an appeal, but South Korean law requires them to start paying even as the appeals process continues, and the fine was booked in 2024, even though the payment plan is covering multiple years, and the company is holding out hope it will be reversed in court entirely.

The big takeaways here are that revenue continued to grow strongly, and they resoundingly beat non-GAAP EPS estimates. The adjusted EBITDA margin remains razor-thin, but it’s improved slightly, so that’s another positive. The non-GAAP P/E is still 89 at the time I’m writing this, so it’s still substantially higher than the sector median, so if this company is to be justified as an investment, it’s going to have to be on the basis of expected growth.

Taiwan and Robots: The Avenues of Earnings Growth

Coupang has two obvious avenues for future improvement in bottom line numbers. Those are expanding the revenue and improving efficiencies. In both of those situations the opportunities are substantial.

Coupang has seen strong growth of its Coupang Taiwan subsidiary, and is also moving toward a restaurant delivery service in Japan, after a previous attempt to establish themselves in Japan was unsuccessful. Taiwan revenue alone grew 23% quarter over quarter in Q4. The biggest growth opportunity however, is probably within South Korea itself.

Though Coupang is nearly a household name there, and styles itself the Amazon of South Korea, it is by no means as dominant as Amazon is the United States. There is plenty of room to improve the business and grow revenue within their primary market, and beyond that, improving efficiency to increase margins is another strategy to make the earnings rise.

And that’s where the robots come in. Coupang is building its business around the idea of efficiency and automation making things ever more convenient for the customers. Those efforts and the relative size of South Korea make it possible to offer same-day delivery, or in the case of night orders, even delivery by dawn to customers. Place the order just before midnight and get it delivered by 7:00 AM, that’s where they’re at in some cases already, and improvements will allow even more products to get delivered that efficiently.

Coupang emphasized their intention to drive margin growth through automation and robots during their most recent conference call, and noted the percent of their infrastructure upgraded to where they want it is only in the low teens right now, so there is ample room for further growth.

Estimates are that in 2025 we’ll see revenue grow to $34.34 billion, and EPS increase to 36¢. In 2026, both get better still, $39.58 billion in revenue and 73¢ in EPS. If things go right, revenue and margin growth could make that happen, and possibly even more. Though this translates to forward P/Es that would still be too high for a value investment, the undeniable momentum in their growth positions them nicely as a growth stock.

Farfetch and Its Prospects

As mentioned above, Coupang acquired Farfetch in January 2024. Farfetch was a world-wide marketplace company that delivers luxury fashion products for various partnered brands and boutiques. Farfetch had quite a substantial customer base, but it was losing hundreds of millions of dollars annually, which is why Coupang was able to buy it for a fairly modest price.

Luckily, Coupang is very experienced at running large marketplaces, and their focus on improving efficiency allowed them to quickly pare away the losses. In the Q4 conference call, they reported reaching effective break-even on Farfetch in just a single year, and with a fairly minimal loss of scale. It’s looking like it could well be a good purchase.

Honestly, Farfetch is probably unlikely to ever be a massive part of Coupang’s portfolio, but if they prove successful in turning it from a big, losing operation into a profitable one, Coupang could well use it as a model for other acquisitions in the future. It’s early to say, but things are looking very promising in that regard.

An American E-Commerce Company In the Age of Tariff Wars

Beyond the risks that a major e-commerce company always faces, Coupang has another substantial risk I would be remiss if I didn’t bring up, the tariff war situation. As I write this, I see that there has been a pause on most of the tariffs, and the market predictably rebounded.

Still, it’s not a resolution, it’s just a pause, and if nothing else changes, we could easily be in the exact same situation 90 days hence. That’s a potential problem for everyone, but potentially a bigger problem for a company like Coupang, which is incorporated in the US, but conducts its operations almost exclusively overseas.

Call it what you like, but this tariff war is leading to a rise in panic and anti-American sentiment across the world. Since the starts and stops of this on-again, off-again crisis are being driven more or less exclusively by the US government, all things American are being viewed in a different light.

Historically, South Korea was very favorable toward the US, because of their role in defense in the Korean War (which 72 years later still hasn’t officially ended because a peace treaty was never signed). The untold billions of dollars this trade war could cost the South Korean economy is a straightforward way that historic goodwill could evaporate, and Coupang’s status as an American company could suddenly become a liability that it wasn’t before.

A Notable Entry Level, But for the Situation

Where does that leave us? It’s complicated.

Coupang has the look of an excellent growth company, and trading on the lower end of their 52-week range, I feel like in normal circumstances I’d rate it a buy. That said, it’s not normal circumstances, and I’m simply going to rate it a hold for now.

Coupang has excellent potential, but the risk of suffering during a global recession caused by whatever comes of this crisis is too great, and we might find an even lower entry point than where we’re at right now, that would make it even more enticing. For now, it’s a watch list stock for sure, but I wouldn’t go any further until we get some clarity on where global trade, and the economy, is headed.

Investors who do jump in right now should keep a close eye on where their adjusted EBITDA margin heads in the next year, and whether they continue to meet expectations with their revenue growth. Either one of those turning away from the direction they’ve been headed is likely the first indication we’ll get that the crisis is doing them substantial harm. In that case, a lower entry point would definitely be better.

data.title: Trump Tariffs Raise Questions In Midstream
data.description:
The Trump Administration has a problem. On the one hand, President Trump has emphasized the importance of "energy dominance"; the maximization of fossil fuels production and the export of American energy globally to exert influence and support allies. On the other hand, he has also criticized domestic gasoline and electricity prices, and has pressured both OPEC and domestic producers to keep prices low.

Exporting energy, all else equal, tightens domestic supply and contributes to higher local prices. Encouraging exports, while it does support job creation and trade balance, works against keeping energy costs down at home. And that's a primary reason why he was elected in the first place. Reconciling those goals is tricky, and we're starting to see the impact of that. Today, I'm going to talk about how that plays into the top companies in the midstream space, and cornerstone midstream firms: Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Targa Resources (TRGP), and Energy Transfer (ET) immediately spring to mind.

Tariff Escalation

The current tariff now sits at 125% on United States exports by China, and quite frankly, given the way this has been going, it could be higher or gone completely by the time I press publish on this research. Originally, Chinese tariffs targeted liquefied natural gas ("LNG") and crude oil. Other energy products like natural gas liquids ("NGLs") were excluded. That now is no longer the case. Tariffs are not the only way these trade flows are impacted. With the United States also considering port fees for Chinese built ships - which is basically a humongous chunk of the seaborne market - costs also would increase just to get the product out of America.

This is a big escalation for China. They import very little crude oil from the United States, and while they are a buyer of American LNG, they have other buying options. NGLs are a different story, specifically propane and ethane, are a different story. Propane is actually one of the highest valued imports into China, and a quarter of all propane that leaves the United States is destined for Chinese ports for use in the production of propylene and cooking / heating. For ethane, Chinese petrochemical crackers that use ethane as a feedstock to create ethylene rely entirely on American exports. These Chinese facilities simply cannot absorb the cost and remain economical versus ethylene produced in other ways. They will likely have to shut down, but given half of waterborne ethane out of the United States ends up in China, it will also significantly disrupt the ethane export industry.

China did not do this without thinking. While it employed similar retaliatory tariffs in 2018 during the last bout of trade tensions, it is much more reliant today. But even back then, Chinese buyers did not absorb the cost - they just scaled back purchases. Rerouting global trade this time around is impossible, but I suspect austerity from China on this issue.

No question, this is a big deal for these three midstream shops. Most know that both companies have substantially committed to the NGL production chain. They dominate the ownership of gas processing plants, NGL pipelines, fractionation at Mont Belvieu, and the ownership of NGL export port infrastructure.

Percent Of Proceed Contracts

Unlike crude oil and natural gas, midstream gas processors most frequently use percent of proceeds ("POP") contracts. When they break down the raw natural gas liquids into their varying separate products, they receive a fee based on the final wholesale value. This makes them price sensitive; if propane and ethane values fall, the money they make falls.

These contracts typically do have fee floors; companies were bumping up against them in late 2023 / early 2024, and that was at prices not all that far away from where we were today. But it's still an unfortunate situation, as higher NGL prices were a big driver of predicted EBITDA improvement for these companies in 2025.

If exports are cut, the question also becomes what happens with the excess product. Even at much lower prices, domestic consumption is not going to rise much, and NGL production was also set to rise materially this year. All of this was coming to leave the country from new export dock expansion (e.g., Enterprise and Morgan's Point expansion, also its new Neches River facility). If cargoes cannot be diverted out of China to other locations, we're going to see volumes back up into domestic storage. Luckily, this is a good time of year to get that volume put into working storage, but if the situation lingers for too long, this could cause issues.

Takeaways

Many pundits had very valid concerns about what would happen to NGL prices before additional export capacity came online, and before this tariff situation, we looked to have dodged that problem. With the issue now fresh again on our minds, investors need to watch what midstream firms with exposure say in upcoming presentations / Q1 conference calls on what exposure they have, how they are protecting their business, and how their 2025 outlooks are changing.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tariff Whipsaw: Mixed Signals And Market Shocks
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Markets remain in flux following President Trump’s announcement of a 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs — excluding China, where import duties were raised sharply. Initially reported at 125%, the White House clarified Thursday that the effective rate on Chinese goods is now 145%. That unexpected revision triggered a sharp reversal in investor sentiment: after Wednesday’s historic rally, U.S. equities fell steeply on Thursday, with the S&P 500 down 3.5%, the Nasdaq dropping 4.3%, and the Dow shedding over 1,000 points.

The initial market surge reflected hopes that the administration was prioritizing financial stability, particularly as bond yields reflected signs of stress. But Thursday’s clarification revealed that policy uncertainty remains elevated. Volatility spiked again, and markets quickly recalibrated as concerns about inflation, policy credibility, and the global growth outlook reemerged.

Even with March’s softer-than-expected CPI data, the economic outlook remains fragile. The U.S. average effective tariff rate is still the highest in the post-World War II era. China's retaliatory actions, including new restrictions on U.S. services imports, add to the risk that global trade slows meaningfully in the months ahead.

For investors, the takeaway is nuanced: policy remains reactive, not resolved. Staying invested, diversified, and flexible across asset classes is critical. After all, markets typically experience several large pullbacks each year. In an environment where political developments are moving faster than fundamentals, resilience matters more than ever.

Original Post
